title: Coffee éclairs
subtitle: Choux pastry fingers filled with coffee crème pâtissière and glazed with dark chocolate
excerpt: Classic éclairs made from choux pastry, filled with a coffee-spiked crème pâtissière and finished with a dark chocolate glaze. The filling is piped in from one end. Makes twelve.
sourceNotes: Éclairs are documented in French patisserie from the mid-nineteenth century onward. They appear under the name éclair au café in Urbain Dubois, La Cuisine Classique (1856), public domain: a choux pastry finger filled with coffee-flavoured cream and glazed with fondant. Jules Gouffé, Le Livre de Pâtisserie (1873), Project Gutenberg-era public domain: éclairs are recorded in both coffee and chocolate variations, filled with crème pâtissière and glazed with fondant or chocolate.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The choux fingers can be baked and stored in an airtight tin for up to 24 hours. Fill and glaze on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 200°C for 20 minutes, then reduce to 170°C for 8 minutes to dry the cases through

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, stirred custard set with cornflour rather than eggs alone. Milk is brought to a simmer, then poured over a mixture of egg yolks, sugar, and cornflour; the combination is returned to the heat and stirred continuously until thick and smooth. A small amount of butter stirred in off the heat gives gloss and richness. The finished cream must be cooled with clingfilm pressed directly onto the surface to prevent a skin forming. It sets firmer than a pouring custard and can be piped.

content.text: The choux fingers must be fully dried in the oven or they will collapse when they cool. The two-stage bake (high then low) achieves this without burning the outside. The
should be cold before piping; warm filling will soften the choux from the inside.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Line two baking trays with paper. Bring the {{whole-milk}} (choux portion), 125 ml water, {{unsalted-butter}} (choux portion), {{caster-sugar}} (choux portion), and {{sea-salt-fine}} to a boil. Remove from the heat, add the {{plain-flour}} all at once, and beat to a smooth paste. Return to medium heat and stir for 2 minutes to dry. Beat in the {{eggs}} (choux portion) one at a time until the paste is smooth and falls in a thick ribbon. Pipe 12 fingers of about 11 cm each onto the trays. Bake for 20 minutes, then reduce to 170°C and bake for a further 8 minutes until firm and deeply golden. Pierce one end of each éclair with a skewer. Cool completely on a rack before filling.

Heat the {{whole-milk}} (crème portion) until steaming. Whisk the {{eggs}} (crème portion), {{caster-sugar}} (crème portion), {{cornflour}}, and dissolved {{coffee-instant}} together in a bowl. Pour the hot milk over the egg mixture in a thin stream, whisking constantly. Return to the pan and stir over medium heat until very thick and bubbling; about 4 minutes. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} (crème portion) until melted. Press clingfilm onto the surface and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes until completely cold.

Fit a piping bag with a small plain nozzle, fill with the cold crème pâtissière, and pipe into each éclair through the hole at the end until you feel resistance. For the glaze: bring the {{double-cream}} just to a simmer, pour over the {{dark-chocolate}}, leave for 1 minute, and stir smooth. Dip the top of each filled éclair into the chocolate glaze. Set on a tray and refrigerate for 20 minutes until the glaze firms. Serve cold.
